games.explanation: Noah Cameron is fine against righties and a problem against lefties, which matters because the Cardinals happen to roll out the exact bats built to punish him. Iván Herrera in particular has been a different hitter against lefties this year, posting a .451 xwOBA that turns a back-end starter into batting practice. Leahy isn't a name, but he's been quietly competent, and the wind blowing in at a warm Kauffman caps the damage Witt and company can do on the other side. At -120, you're laying short juice on the better team with the better matchup and the better weather read.

Sanchez has been one of the quieter problems in the National League all year, but the lefty-on-lefty trick that powers his numbers stops working when Pittsburgh runs a lineup full of right-handed bats at him. Against righties his xwOBA jumps nearly seventy points, and Ozuna in particular is the kind of patient power bat that doesn't chase the changeup off the plate. Bubba Chandler is the wild card, but the projected score is essentially a one-run game the market has decided is a comfortable Philly win. At +148 on a coin flip that isn't actually a coin flip, you take the points and let the matchup cook.

Sandy Alcantara still has the name recognition of an ace, but the underlying profile this year is a mid-rotation arm getting barreled at a rate that doesn't survive a real lineup. Tampa happens to bring that lineup, with Aranda and Caminero leading a right-handed group posting hard-hit numbers north of 50% against righties, and Alcantara's stuff has played down to a pedestrian .332 xwOBA-against. Nick Martinez quietly outperforms him on contact quality, and the Elo gap here is a full 200 points in Tampa's favor. The market is pricing the logo on the jersey, not the season that's actually been pitched.

The board is treating this like Cleveland is meaningfully better, and they just aren't. These are two teams separated by a rounding error in the ratings, the sim spits out a one-run game, and you're getting paid plus money to take the side everyone is reflexively fading. Paddack is hittable, sure, but Elly and a Sal Stewart bat that has been barreling everything in sight against righties give Cincinnati a real path through the Cleveland staff. Wind blowing in at 76 degrees points to exactly the kind of tight game where the underdog price stops making sense. +154 on a coin flip is the entire pitch.

A coin-flip price on the Cubs against the White Sox is the kind of mistake the board only makes when it's anchored to Davis Martin's tidy-looking surface line. Read another column and Martin is a contact-management story waiting to unravel, with a barrel rate against that screams regression and a left-handed split that turns Busch and Happ into problems he can't pitch around. Taillon is quietly the much steadier arm in this game, and the Cubs are the better team by a wide margin before you even get to the lineup edge. Paying -104 for the side our numbers make a near-58% favorite is the entire pitch.

The market is treating this like Gilbert's name on the marquee should scare San Diego off, ignoring that the Padres run out the kind of lineup that doesn't particularly care who's on the mound. Tatis and Machado both punish right-handed pitching at the top of the order, and Gilbert's xwOBA against righties is closer to ordinary than the strikeout numbers suggest. Buehler is the obvious wart here, but the simulation still spits out San Diego as the better team by a comfortable margin in a closed-roof game that should play cleanly. Getting +141 on the better roster against a starter who's good, not untouchable, is the entire pitch.

Boston is rolling out a rookie lefty with 75 batters faced in the majors against a Braves lineup built to punish exactly that, and the market is treating it like a coin flip. Tolle is walking nearly 11% of hitters and getting tagged for a .347 xwOBA by righties, which is a problem when Olson and Baldwin are waiting in the middle of the order. Elder is nothing special, but he doesn't need to be when the opposing arm is this raw and the park is playing neutral with the wind blowing in. At -140 you're laying juice on the better team, the better lineup, and the more experienced starter, which is usually how this is supposed to work.

Jose Soriano walks too many hitters to be standing across from this lineup at a price that implies a competitive game. The Dodgers are the better team by a wide margin, they're running out the sharper arm, and Soriano's command issues are exactly the wrong profile to bring into a Friday night with Ohtani waiting in the box. A double-digit walk rate against right-handed bats turns into free baserunners for the best hitter on the planet, and Wrobleski has quietly been one of the more efficient strike-throwers in the Dodger rotation. -135 against a sub-.500 Halos club feels like the market still pricing the logo on the cap rather than the names on the mound.

Philadelphia is the better team and probably wins this game, but the run line is asking the wrong question, because Sanchez is a contact-managing lefty who doesn't miss enough bats to bury a lineup on his own. The Pirates happen to roll out a right-handed top of the order that fares meaningfully better against him, with Reynolds and Ozuna both posting xwOBAs above .330 versus lefties. Chandler is no ace, but he's been competent enough to keep a Schwarber-Harper lineup honest in a park that quietly suppresses homers (HR factor 0.81 tonight). The sim lands on a one-run game roughly three out of five times, and -115 to catch every version of that is the entire pitch.
